why: delete_api_key_route resolves and deletes the API key solely by the caller-supplied api_key_id with no check that the key belongs to the authenticated user.
build_flow has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_active_user) in its decorator), allowing unauthenticated callers to trigger arbitrary flow builds.
get_build_events retrieves build events for any caller-supplied job_id with no check that the job belongs to the authenticated user.
cancel_build cancels a job identified solely by the caller-supplied job_id with no ownership check to ensure it belongs to the authenticated user.
traceback.format_exc() is captured into tb and returned to the client inside the VertexBuildResponse outputs, exposing full stack traces including internal paths and component details.

webhook_run_flow has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_active_user) or Depends(api_key_security)); get_webhook_user may allow unauthenticated execution depending on auth settings, meaning any external caller can trigger arbitrary flow execution.

The Content-Disposition header is built by interpolating file_name (a caller-supplied path parameter) directly into the header value without stripping CR/LF or properly quoting the filename, enabling header injection.

create_upload_file accepts a flow_id and saves a file to a folder named after it but has no dependency enforcing that the authenticated user owns that flow, allowing any authenticated user to upload files into any flow's storage folder.

The exception from verify_public_flow_and_get_user or start_flow_build (which may originate in a database/ORM layer and embed SQL, connection strings, or absolute paths) is converted with str(exc) and returned as the HTTPException detail to the unauthenticated caller.
download_image has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_user) or equivalent), allowing any unauthenticated caller to read arbitrary stored files by guessing flow_id and file_name.
download_profile_picture has no authentication dependency, so any unauthenticated caller can retrieve files from the filesystem.
list_profile_pictures has no authentication dependency, exposing the directory listing to unauthenticated callers.

read_public_flow has no authentication dependency and only checks access_type == PUBLIC, but the comparison `if access_type is not AccessTypeEnum.PUBLIC` using identity (`is not`) instead of equality (`!=`) may fail for non-singleton enum values, potentially exposing non-public flows.

The Content-Disposition header is built using an f-string that interpolates `filename`, which is derived from the current datetime and is not user-controlled here, but the flow name at line 637 is user-controlled and embedded in the zip entry name without CR/LF stripping; note that the filename in the header is time-based and safe, but this is borderline — flagging for review.
read_basic_examples has no authentication dependency, allowing any unauthenticated caller to read all starter folder flows.

The `search` query parameter (external user input) is interpolated directly into the redirect_url without URL-encoding or stripping of CR/LF characters, allowing header injection via the Location header of the RedirectResponse.

kb_name is taken directly from the URL path parameter and joined to the user directory via '/' without any realpath/resolve + containment check, so a value like '../other_user/secret' can escape the intended user-scoped directory.

kb_name from the URL path is appended to kb_user_path without canonicalization or containment check, allowing '../' traversal to delete directories outside the authenticated user's space, including other users' knowledge bases.

Each kb_name in the bulk-delete request body is joined to kb_user_path without resolve/commonpath containment check, allowing an attacker to supply '../victim_user/kb' values to delete arbitrary directories.
The DELETE handler for /{folder_id} has no authentication dependency (no Depends(get_current_user) or equivalent), allowing any unauthenticated caller to trigger a delete redirect for any folder_id.
The GET /download/{folder_id} handler has no authentication dependency, allowing unauthenticated callers to trigger a download redirect for any folder_id.
The user's store_api_key is written into the 'apikey_tkn_lflw' cookie in plaintext and returned to the client, exposing a sensitive API key credential without encryption or redaction.
In auto_login, the user's store_api_key is set in the 'apikey_tkn_lflw' cookie in plaintext, exposing a sensitive credential to the client without encryption or redaction.

auto_login has no authentication dependency and issues long-term access tokens when AUTO_LOGIN is enabled; the endpoint is reachable by any unauthenticated caller, effectively granting them authenticated access.
The BrokenResourceError exception message (which may contain internal transport details such as host/port) is interpolated directly into the HTTPException detail returned to the client.

The broad except block at line 287 converts the raw exception to str(e) and returns it as the HTTPException detail, potentially leaking database query text, file paths, or connection strings from ORM or filesystem errors.
If an exception occurs after decrypting auth_settings (line 277), the raw exception—which may contain decrypted credential values—is serialised into the HTTP response detail via str(e).

The client IP is read from the untrusted X-Forwarded-For header without validating that the request passed through a trusted proxy, allowing an attacker to spoof a loopback address and bypass the local-only check in install_mcp_config.

install_mcp_config relies on get_client_ip which trusts X-Forwarded-For, meaning a remote attacker can forge a local IP in that header and bypass the is_local_ip guard that restricts config installation to local connections.

The caught Exception 'e' from an internal MCP Composer startup operation is passed directly as detail=str(e) to HTTPException with status 500, potentially exposing internal error messages, paths, or connection details to the caller.
The broad except handler at line 864 raises HTTPException with detail=str(e), leaking raw exception text from file I/O, JSON parsing, subprocess, or API key creation operations to the caller.
The outer except at line 1032 raises HTTPException with detail=str(e), potentially exposing internal error messages from database, filesystem, or network operations to the caller.

The Windows username obtained from cmd.exe stdout is interpolated directly into a filesystem path without stripping path-separator characters or validating it against an allowlist, allowing a username like '../../etc' to traverse outside the intended directory.

The generated Langflow API key (langflow_api_key) is placed directly into the args list that is written in plaintext to the MCP JSON config file on disk, exposing the secret outside the application's secret management boundary.

The filename is extracted from a caller-supplied URI via URL-decoding (unquote) and passed directly to storage_service.get_file without any path containment check, allowing a crafted URI with '../' sequences to escape the intended directory.

The raw exception message from flow execution (which may embed SQL, file paths, or internal state) is appended directly to collected_results and returned to the MCP caller as TextContent, leaking internal error details to the external client.

The exception string from the database/ORM/flow execution layer is interpolated into an error message returned directly to the MCP client via TextContent, potentially exposing SQL queries, file paths, or connection strings.

The caller-supplied `order_by` string is passed directly to `getattr(MessageTable, order_by)` without an allowlist, allowing an attacker to reference arbitrary model attributes and potentially trigger ORM-level errors or unexpected column access; while not classic string concatenation, it is unvalidated external input controlling query structure.

The handler deletes messages by caller-supplied `message_ids` without verifying that any of those message records belong to the authenticated user, allowing an authenticated user to delete any other user's messages.
The handler fetches and mutates a MessageTable row solely by the caller-supplied `message_id` path parameter with no check that the message belongs to the authenticated user.
Messages are fetched and updated using the caller-supplied `old_session_id` with no ownership check verifying those messages belong to the authenticated user's flows.
Messages are deleted by caller-supplied `session_id` without verifying that those messages belong to the authenticated user's flows.
Transactions are fetched by caller-supplied `flow_id` with no check that the flow belongs to the authenticated user, exposing any user's transaction data.

The raw exception `str(e)` from internal flow execution (which may include file paths, connection strings, or SQL details) is serialised into the SSE stream and returned to the client via `create_openai_error(message=str(e), ...)`.
The raw exception string `str(exc)` from flow execution is embedded in the OpenAI error response body returned to the client, potentially leaking internal paths, SQL, or connection details.

On decryption failure the function falls back to returning the raw (still-encrypted or possibly plaintext) store_api_key value to the caller instead of returning None or raising, exposing the secret field.

The flows query at line 604 fetches all flows by folder_id without scoping to current_user.id, so an authenticated user who owns the project can retrieve flows belonging to other users if folder_id ownership check passes but individual flow ownership differs; more critically the pattern leaks flows not scoped to the authenticated user.

The Content-Disposition header is built using an f-string that interpolates encoded_filename derived from project.name (a database/user-supplied value); although quote() percent-encodes non-ASCII characters it does not strip CR/LF or other header-breaking characters that could split the header.

The exception from validate_code (which may invoke compile/ast and emit SQL, file paths, or internal details) is converted to str(e) and returned as the HTTP 500 detail, leaking internal error context to the caller.

The raw exception from process_prompt_template is forwarded as str(e) in the HTTP 500 detail, potentially leaking internal implementation details such as template parsing errors with internal paths or field names.

update_variable_fields is called with the caller-supplied variable_id and current_user.id, but whether the service layer enforces that the variable belongs to current_user depends on the service implementation; if the service does not filter by user_id when resolving variable_id, any authenticated user can update any variable by ID.

delete_variable_by_id is called with the caller-supplied variable_id alongside current_user.id, but without a visible ownership check in the handler itself confirming the variable belongs to the authenticated user before deletion.

The plaintext OpenAI API key (openai_key) is returned from this function and passed to callers, and its value originates from either the variable service or the environment; if downstream code logs or exposes this value, the secret is leaked without redaction.
The JSON decode error exception message (e!s) is forwarded directly to the OpenAI/client WebSocket as a function_call_output, potentially leaking internal parsing details derived from external input.
The ValueError exception message (e!s) is forwarded directly into the function_call_output sent over the WebSocket, leaking internal error details to the caller.
The ConnectionError/WebSocketException message (e!s) including potential host/port details is forwarded into the function_call_output sent to the client WebSocket.
The KeyError/AttributeError/TypeError exception message is forwarded into the function_call_output sent to the client, leaking internal attribute or key names.

exec() is called with a function body (func_body) built by interpolating input_.display_name values (lines 280, 293–296) directly into a Python source string without sanitization or allowlisting; those display names are user-controlled flow-vertex names stored in the database, so a crafted name (e.g. containing newlines and arbitrary Python) is executed with full globals().

get_flow_desc_from_db is called with the caller-supplied path parameter flow_id (line 701/718) with no check that the resolved flow belongs to current_user; only user authentication is performed (line 731–734), not resource ownership verification.

build_flow_and_stream is invoked with the caller-supplied path parameter flow_id converted to UUID (line 1255) without any prior check that the flow belongs to current_user; authentication is present (line 1200–1201) but no ownership assertion is made before the flow is built and streamed.

A requests.RequestException—a network-client exception whose message commonly embeds the full URL, host, port, or connection details—is converted via str(e) and returned verbatim to the caller as {"error": str(e)}, exposing internal network topology.
